item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Apollyon is not an official Object Class so it depends on the author whether they want to use it or not.

That said, in this case, there is actually a tale (also part of the antimemetics series) that explains why this isn't Apollyon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: shouldn't this be apollyon?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just wanted to say to the author(s) of this SCP,
This is dope and I appreciate your contribution with this.
It has given me some ideas,
If you're interesting in reading them they are located at this thread -> Idea inspired by SCP-3125 and The Security Clearance Levels

Keep up the good work!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: epic

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What you're missing is that somebody would need to implement this brainwashing, order the parts, separate the parts they do need from those they don't, give the build orders, etc. And how would you get them to do that without a single person in that command staff even once doing any critical thinking on why they're doing it? If we're brainwashing the brainwashers, then it's turtles all the way down. They don't have the infrastructure to build the infrastructure, it's Catch-22.

See also CASE COLOURLESS GREEN, earlier in this Tale series.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I read through the whole five five five five five series to try and figure out the code, and it was right in plain sight on the canon hub the whole time.

item.title: Re: quesion
item.content:encoded: It's the character used in the file and related tales to refer to SCP-3125. In the Telugu language, it is pronounced "va" - but mostly it is character 3125 in Unicode, via hexadecimal coding.

item.title: quesion
item.content:encoded: does the symbol that appears on the keypad wen you get the combo wrong mean anything

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I meditate - why this object is not Appolyon or Tiamat? First explanation is that author so hate esoteric classes, what he not use them even where justified. But, I came up with another explanation. This object is Keter because really it is not more dangerous than another usual, medium Keter. Why? Because in fact the object is not everywhere except the container. Object is contain in container, and information, what staff read in container is false information. And this information is memetic-hazard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nah, man. You're supposed to go

OH GOD OH FUCK

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: SCP-055. Same author.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This nightmare seems like a logical evolution of that super old SCP that a couple of soldiers are trying to fight without being able to remember what it is, other than it's not a sphere. The way it has eaten the world's vast majority of memetics researchers and left no evidence is terrifying. Especially, I think, the way that it automatically attacks the only people capable of doing anything about it, or indeed knowing that something even needs to be done about it. Yikes. +1 for sure.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
as well as requiring tremendous material resources, this machine could not be constructed without its builders understanding why it was being built, which would require an understanding of SCP-3125, which would prove fatal to the project.

I'm not sure if that's true. You might need to train or somehow brainwash a bunch of construction workers such that they will follow the blueprints exactly without cutting corners, asking questions, or thinking critically. And you might need to cover completed sections with tarps and amnesticize the workers every time they complete a new section of it. And of course you'd want to produce the parts offsite and have them shipped in secret through front companies with a bunch of bogus part orders to obfuscate what it is you're actually building. But in principle, there's no reason why a person needs to know what a machine is for or even understand how a machine works in order to physically attach a bunch of parts to each other to build said machine.

Tab A. Slot B. No questions. Now take your pills. Repeat until we tell you to stop.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Interesting crosslink between the Addendum 2517-1 (in SCP-2517) and this anomaly. Intriguing details provided in tales crosslinking this SCP as well. By itself, the SCP still stands strong and interesting even down to the concept.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: SCP-3125000 through 3125999? (SCP-3,125,437 is the best of that series and deserves to be more well-known)

item.content:encoded: That is an absolutely superb analogy, qntm. The perfec

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Perhaps SCP-31(Theta-Prime)5?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pleased to be the 555th upvote on this terrifying head-scratcher

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: God damn, mine was 42069

item.title: Re: Idea
item.content:encoded:
Well there are two questions there, one is whether SCP-3125 counts as Apollyon and the other is whether I should list it as such in the writeup.

As I understand it, "Apollyon" means "completely uncontainable, and will 100% certainly wipe out all of humanity no matter what we do". I think SCP-3125 falls marginally short of this definition. As related in this document, there is at least one method by which it is believed that SCP-3125 could theoretically be stopped. Additionally, the existence of the SCP-3125 "inverted containment unit" itself indicates that there are ways to keep SCP-3125 out of your universe. It's an extremely bleak outlook, but it's a long way short of hopeless.

The other angle is whether, in-universe, the term "Apollyon" would be used here, even if it were appropriate. I think it wouldn't, for a simple reason: the Apollyon classification is a confession of defeat. And at the time of writing, the Antimemetics Division was dying, but not defeated. To call SCP-3125 "Apollyon" would alter SCP-3125's perception among the Antimemetics Division for the worse, elevate SCP-3125's power, harm Division morale and decrease the chance of ever containing SCP-3125. Writing "Keter" instead of "Apollyon" could, in fact, be viewed as a tiny act of defiance and confidence.

To be honest I can see this being a legitimate in-universe reason for the Foundation (outside the Antimemetics Division, the Foundation in general) to have coined the "Apollyon" classification and then thought better of it and retired it. You can imagine an email circulating, saying: "'Apollyon' is a confession of defeat, and the Foundation is not a suitable working environment for defeatists." I may even write that Tale.

item.title: Idea
item.content:encoded: Change the object class to Apollyon, I think it suits it more. BUT only include it in the full report. This is based on everyting i have read, which is everything, from the antimemetics division, wait what is the antimemetics division, do we even have one…

item.content:encoded: The "+" is, yes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
and SCP+█████

Is it intended?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The entire antimemetics series is why i registered on this site.

I wish i had more than a single upvote to give. Fantastic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nice scripting. This must've taken some work. I've been using the CSS from here for some of my pages. I hope you don't mind. The writing's good too.

+1

Fun fact: I actually borrowed an early version of the keypad from qntm like in January, to spice up my sandbox. Still surprised that you can code a literal keypad like that in Wikidot.

Oh, and the skips pretty cool. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is that a reply to my post?

I would guess you could do something like that using javascript; perhaps constantly loading a new image scaled down to not be visible, or load a heavy page inside a frame that isn't visible and keeps refreshing the contents of the frame, or maybe keep reloading some small amount of data from a server programmed to pretend to be slow (don't forget to mark the data as non-cacheable, so it can't be loaded from the previous local copy); or you could just fake it and leave the contents of the entry empty and override the page's favicon with the gif of a throbber like what some browsers do while loading a page. Or maybe there is some cleaner way to do it with JS or some server tweaks that don't involve infinitely downloading data but still keeps the browser acting as if the page hasn't finished loading; you would have to ask someone that is more familiar with these things.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Woah, how'd you do that?

item.title: It was not what it looked like at first
item.content:encoded: I was gonna compliment the genius of making an antimeme skip page have trouble loading completely; but then I read a few of the comments here, and upon further investigation it turns out that wasn't intentional and I just had to allow a couple of additional things in my ad-blocker…

item.title: Re: Organization Q's
item.content:encoded:
Why are there two Special Containment Procedure paragraphs?

You have to think "in-universe" here. just like you can search the wiki, a foundation employee can search their database for SCP objects. maybe they even have the containment procedures written on a plaque or clipboard hanging at the door to the containment chamber

The "upper" SCP also includes information about the make up of the chamber and the airlock, which might be important for maintenance teams.

But it is also meant as a decoy, so no one who is not a "senior Antimemetics Division staff member" will ask questions.

In the tale it is implied that the "real" containment procedure and description are inside the chamber, meaning behind the door with the keypad. so you would never know there is a second procedure or description if you were not inside once.

The reason for doing this with the keypad and not just a collapsible (with "requires X clearance to view") is most likely to put some attention to the corresponding tales. but it's also some nifty HTML coding.

item.title: Organization Q's
item.content:encoded: This is really cool and I love the keypad! But there is something I'm confused about: Why are there two Special Containment Procedure paragraphs. Also why not just lock access to the entire article until they enter the key code. Just a suggestion!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: FireFox under Windows 10, once I've entered the code and gotten to the main article, clicking an addendum kicks me back to the code entry page.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is excellent. and I'm going to try and not lose any sleep about how I got onto the SCP from the "highest rated" page, entered 55555 by default into the keypad and just figured it let you in no matter what you put. I'm just gonna upvote and hide under my bed for a bit.

item.title: Re: Think I cracked the cipher you used to encode your text
item.content:encoded: Yes, that string "owtnsfvlnqyfzbdercgqiuapucjekhamblshwoxpgzyrttxkmi" is right there in the source of the page in fact.

item.title: Think I cracked the cipher you used to encode your text
item.content:encoded: Awesome SCP btw but I wanted to try to crack your cipher. You encoded your text with a Vignere cipher with the key: owtnsfvlnqyfzbdercgqiuapucjekhamblshwoxpgzyrttxkmi. I was hoping that it'd be some cool easter egg but ah well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What OS and browser? Anything in the console?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hey, I put in the code, and nothing appeared. Any idea as to why?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Will fix.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I came here from CASE COLORLESS GREEN, and as such was not expecting that third addendum. Without it, I'd have been hovering on the edge of an upvote; once I read it, I jumped up top and started hammering on the +1 button. Well played.

+ Unrelatedly, a nitpick.
– hide block

(Here, "our own" refers to human head space; the set of all ideas which humans have or are biologically capable of having.)

This should be a full colon, not a semicolon. (The second half of the sentence isn't an independent clause.) I'd fix it myself, but encryption.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Belatedly, are you on mobile? This works fine for me on desktops/laptops, but none of my mobile devices will open it. Try it on a full computer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: At the point of when I saw this originally, I was doing down to click the Discuss link when I saw the Antimemetic tag.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yahoo! More Antimemetic Division stuff!

Super, super happy to see this. I absolutely adored the Antimemetics Division series, it's definitely my favourite. Something different that I like about this (that is, aside from everything else that I loved before this was published) is the different perspective. As someone said above, the tales give a personal viewpoint of 3125, whereas this is the actual scientific document, focusing on the facts rather than the emotions, so the whole thing gets the Foundation's clinical treatment, which I really like.

Unfortunately, my inner Grammar Nazi cannot be contained:
– hide block

Marion Wheeler, chief of Antimemetics

I feel like 'chief' should be capitalised, as it's a title.

you put it into action it more than once.

You got an extra 'it' at the end there. EDIT: This has now been fixed, which means: Yay, I helped!
Right, no more.

Antother thing I love, absolutely LOVE, is all the hidden little features, from 5 fives being in the fist section, to the trick with the name 'The Escapee', I assume that was intentional. The most curious thing was the symbol Marion Wheeler used to refer to 3125: 'వ'. Now what is that? I looked it up, to find it's a Telegu letter and noun. As a letter it transliterates as 'th', and is pronounced 'va'. As a noun it's short for 'వచనము', pronounced 'vacanamu', which appears to be something to do with speech, speaking, words and sentences.
But that's probably not why you chose it. As I was researching it, I found that in Unicode it has the reference code '0C35', which, in hexadecimal, is 3125. Neat. So your choice probably had nothing to do with Telegu, and now my head hurts from looking at all those symbols, thanks for that. Still, an amazing little detail, hats off to you.

In all, super pleased right now, just what I needed to stimulate my brain as I reach the drudging, grating end of my exam period. It's stuff like this that makes me love this site. Looking forward to the Five Five Five Five Five series!

item.title: Wow
item.content:encoded:
My accidental guessing of the code reaffirms my "stumble your way to success" outlook on life.

Also, I was not aware (heh) there was a developed antimemetics hub. 55 has always been one of my favorites.

Absolutely enjoyed this scip. Now off to that hub to get some backstory.

Edit: My word. Absolutely excellent. I appreciate that keypad significantly more after reading the tales.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
so, i just built a way to decrypt this, was quite easy and i thought I was cool, 2 minutes later I find out, that the code is already known. If you would like to see my """"hacked"""" version, I can send it to you. I basically used the code against itself.
Well a lot of time wasted, but i wanted to tell you that this was really awesome. Haven't had this much fun in some time

item.title: (no title)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It would be if people guessed randomly. 5 is in the middle of the keypad, and it's easy to click repeatedly… I could see it being the first pick for someone taking potshots at the problem. It's like making "password" your password.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The odds of doing so on your first try are 10-5
